notes.section_title: Introduction: India as an Ancient Land

content.text: Today, the India we know is a modern nation, with defined borders, defined states and a known population. However, 500 years ago, 2,000 years ago or even 5,000 years ago, it was very different. This region of the world, which we often call the 'Indian Subcontinent', has had many different names and shifting boundaries. We can learn about India's past and evolution from many different sources including ancient texts, accounts of travelers and pilgrims, inscriptions, and contemporary historical research. The idea of India as a unified nation is relatively modern, but the geographical and cultural concept of 'India' has existed for thousands of years.

content.title: Key Concept: India as Evolving Entity
content.text: India's identity as a political nation (with defined borders) is modern (post-independence 1947), but India as a cultural and geographical concept extends back thousands of years. Ancient texts identified regions, peoples, and religious traditions that constitute 'India.' The modern nation-state emerged from this historical and cultural continuity.

The Indian Subcontinent is bounded by natural features: the Himalayas in the north, the Arabian Sea in the west, the Bay of Bengal in the east, and the Indian Ocean to the south. These geographical boundaries have remained relatively consistent throughout history. The presence of major river systems (Indus, Ganges, Brahmaputra, and others) created fertile regions that supported early civilizations and continue to sustain large populations.

The Ṛig Veda is India's most ancient text; it is several thousand years old. It gives the northwest region of the Subcontinent the name 'Sapta Sindhava', that is, the 'land of the seven rivers'. The word 'Sindhava' comes from 'Sindhu', which refers to the Indus River, or at times to a river in general. This ancient terminology shows that even in very early times, the geographical features (particularly rivers) were central to how people understood and named the land.

content.title: Sapta Sindhava: The First Named Region
content.text: Sapta Sindhava (land of seven rivers) is the earliest documented name for part of the Subcontinent. The Rig Veda, dated to approximately 1500 BCE or earlier, used this term for the northwest region with its river systems. The naming reflects the importance of rivers for agriculture, transportation, and sustenance.

Moving forward in time, we see names appear in the literature. The Mahābhārata is one of India's most famous texts. Interestingly, it lists many regions, such as Kāshmīra (more or less today's Kashmir), Kurukṣhetra (parts of Haryana today), Vanga (parts of Bengal), Prāgjyotiṣha (roughly today's Assam), Kaccha (today's Kutch), and Kerala (more or less today's Kerala), and so on. When scholars try to identify a name for the entire Indian Subcontinent, the Mahābhārata uses the terms 'Bhāratavarṣha' and 'Jambudvīpa'. Scholars generally agree that this long poem was written from a few centuries BCE onward.

content.title: Bhāratavarṣha: Country of the Bharatas
content.text: Bhāratavarṣha clearly extends to the entire Subcontinent, and the text includes the names of numerous rivers and peoples. 'Bhāratavarṣha' means 'the country of the Bharatas'. 'Bhārata' is a name that first appears in the Ṛig Veda, where it refers to one of the main Vedic groups of people. In later literature, several kings named 'Bharata' are mentioned, giving additional layers to the name's meaning.

content.title: Jambudvīpa: Island of the Jamun Tree
content.text: Jambudvīpa means 'the island of the jamun fruit tree'. This is indeed a common tree native to India, also called 'jambal tree', 'Malabar plum tree', etc. The term came to mean the Indian Subcontinent. This name reflects how geographical features and flora were integrated into India's self-identification.

We get a good clue from an Indian emperor — his name is Aśhoka and we will meet him later; for now, we can take his date to be about 250 BCE. As we will see, he left us many inscriptions. In one of them, he used the same name 'Jambudvīpa' to describe the whole of India, which at the time included what is today Bangladesh, Pakistan, as well as parts of Afghanistan. This shows that in Aśhoka's time (3rd century BCE), the term Jambudvīpa was used to refer to the entire geographical and political extent of the Indian Subcontinent as it was understood then.

content.title: Aśhoka's Empire and Terminology
content.text: Emperor Aśhoka's use of Jambudvīpa in inscriptions around 250 BCE provides concrete historical evidence that this term was used for the entire Subcontinent. His empire's extent (including modern Pakistan, Bangladesh, and parts of Afghanistan) corresponded to what was called Jambudvīpa, showing the geographical scope of the name's application.

A few centuries later, 'Bhārata' became the name generally used for the Indian Subcontinent. For instance, in an ancient Purāṇa, we read: samudrasya himādreścaiva uttaram yat dakṣiṇam vā — 'The country that lies north of the ocean and south of the snowy mountains is called Bhārata.' This name, 'Bhārata' remains in use even today. In north India, it is generally written as 'Bharat', while in south India, it is often 'Bhāratam'.

content.title: Bhārata as National Identity
content.text: Bhārata became the primary name for India in Indian languages. The Puranic definition based on geographical boundaries (north of ocean, south of Himalayas) provided a stable conceptual framework for understanding 'India' that transcended political changes. This name continues in modern usage — 'Bharat' in Hindi, 'Bhārata' in Sanskrit.

It is interesting to note that different parts of the country adopted a similar definition for India. For instance, a poem of ancient Tamil literature, from about 2,000 years ago, praises a king whose name is known 'from [Cape] Kumari in the south, from the great mountain in the north, from the oceans on the east and on the west.' You can now recognise 'the great mountain in the north', and it should not be difficult to identify 'Cape Kumari'. It looks like ancient Indians knew their geography well! This shows that whether in north or south, east or west, Indians used similar geographical markers (mountains, oceans, rivers) to define the extent of 'India'.

The first foreigners to mention India were the ancient inhabitants of Iran. In the 6th century BCE, a Persian emperor launched a military campaign and gained control of the region of the Indus River, which, as we saw, was earlier called 'Sindhu'. So, it is no surprise that in their earliest records and stone inscriptions, the Persians referred to India as 'Hind', 'Hidu' or 'Hindu', which are adaptations in their language of 'Sindhu'. Note that in ancient Persian, the word 'Hindu' is a purely geographical term; it does not refer here to the Hindu religion.

content.title: Cause-Effect: Geography Determines Foreign Names
content.text: CAUSE: Persians controlled the Indus (Sindhu) River region in 6th century BCE. EFFECT: They named the region after this river, calling it 'Hind'/'Hidu'/'Hindu' (adaptations of Sindhu). This shows how geographical features accessible to foreigners shaped their naming of India.

topics.topic_name: Greek and Latin Adaptations: Indoi, Indike, India

content.text: Based on these Persian sources, the ancient Greeks named the region 'Indoi' or 'Indike'. They dropped the initial letter 'h' of 'Hindu' because this letter did not exist in their Greek language. From Greek sources, the Romans eventually derived the name 'India' for the region, which became the standard Latin and later English term for the country. This etymological chain shows how the name evolved through different languages and cultures as they encountered and named the Subcontinent.

content.title: Etymology: Sanskrit to Greek to Latin to English
content.text: Sindhu (Sanskrit for Indus River) → Hindu (Persian, geographical) → Indoi/Indike (Greek, dropping 'h') → India (Latin/English, standard form). This linguistic journey reflects the historical process of how the Subcontinent became known to the outside world, initially through trade and conquest, with the Indus River being the primary geographical feature that foreigners encountered.

The ancient Chinese also interacted with India. In several texts, they refer to India as 'Yintu' or 'Yindu'. This word also originally comes from 'Sindhu'. Another Chinese word, also derived from 'Sindhu', was 'Tianzhu'; but this word could also be understood as 'heavenly master'. This reflects the respect the ancient Chinese had for India as the land of the Buddha, suggesting that China knew India not just as a geographical region but as a center of spiritual significance.

content.title: Chinese Perspective: Geography and Spirituality
content.text: Chinese names for India reflected both geographical and cultural understanding. Yintu/Yindu came from Sindhu (geographical), while Tianzhu (heavenly master) reflected India's significance as the birthplace of Buddhism. This shows that different cultures named India based on their own encounters and understandings.

topics.topic_name: Xuanzang: A Chinese Scholar's Journey to India

content.text: Xuanzang (formerly spelt Hiuen Tsang, Hsuan Tsang, etc.) travelled from China to India in the 7th century CE. He visited many parts of India, met scholars, collected Buddhist texts, and returned to China after 17 years. There, he translated the manuscripts he took back with him from Sanskrit into Chinese. Several other Chinese scholars visited India over the centuries, facilitating cultural and intellectual exchange between the two civilizations.

content.title: Xuanzang's Significance
content.text: Xuanzang's 17-year journey and his translation of Sanskrit texts into Chinese exemplify the deep cultural exchanges between India and China. His work preserved Buddhist knowledge and created intellectual bridges between civilizations. His travels and writings also provide historical documentation of India during the 7th century CE.

You are probably quite familiar with a more recent term, 'Hindustān', but you may not know that it was first used in a Persian inscription some 1,800 years ago! Later on, this became the term used by most invaders of the Indian Subcontinent. The term 'Hindustān' combines 'Hindu' (the geographical designation for the region) with the Persian suffix '-stān' (meaning 'land of'), creating 'land of the Hindus' or 'the Hindu land'. This term gained prominence with Islamic invaders and rulers from Central Asia and Persia.

content.title: Hindustān: Persian-Islamic Nomenclature
content.text: Hindustān emerged as a term in the medieval period, used by Persian and Islamic rulers. The term -stān suffix is common in Persian and is found in many modern country names (Afghanistan, Pakistan, Kazakhstan). During the Islamic period of Indian history, Hindustān was the standard term for the Subcontinent.

topics.topic_name: From Ancient Times to Modern Constitution

content.text: The multiplicity of names for India reflects the diversity of peoples and cultures that have called this land home, as well as the perspectives of those who encountered it from outside. From the Ṛig Veda's Sapta Sindhava to the Mahābhārata's Bhāratavarṣha and Jambudvīpa, from the Persian Hind to the Greek Indoi, from the Chinese Yintu to the Islamic Hindustān — each name tells a story about who named the land and from which perspective it was named.

content.title: Key Concept: Names Reflect Perspectives and Encounters
content.text: Internal names (Bhārata, Jambudvīpa) were based on Indians' understanding of their own land — cultural, religious, and geographical. External names (Hind, Indoi, India) were based on foreigners' encounters — often through trade, conquest, or travel, frequently anchored in the Indus River which was the primary point of foreign contact.

topics.topic_name: The Indian Constitution's Phrase: 'India, That Is Bharat'

content.text: The Indian Constitution, which was first written in English, uses the phrase 'India, that is Bharat' right at the beginning. Similarly, the Hindi version of the Constitution mentions the same concept as 'Bhārat arthāth India'. This phrase in the modern Constitution beautifully encapsulates India's historical journey. 'India' is the name given to the land by foreigners, while 'Bharat' is the name given by its own people. By using both names together, the Constitution acknowledges both the internal cultural identity and the external/international recognition of the nation.

content.title: Constitutional Significance of 'India, That Is Bharat'
content.text: The Constitution's opening phrase symbolizes India's dual identity — India (as known to the world, derived from foreign encounters and Western understanding) and Bharat (as known to its own people, derived from ancient Sanskrit texts and cultural continuity). Using both names simultaneously shows that the modern nation encompasses both its ancient indigenous identity and its place in the modern international system.

notes.section_title: Table of Names: A Summary of Evolution

rows: Land of the Hindus (-stān means land of)

content.title: Patterns in Naming
content.text: Internal names (by Indians) emphasize cultural identity (Bhāratavarṣha, Jambudvīpa) and connect to foundational texts. External names tend to derive from the Indus River (Sindhu → Hindu → Indoi → India) because foreigners primarily encountered this region. Modern India uses both — India internationally, Bharat nationally — reflecting its dual identity in the modern world.

The Indian Subcontinent is defined by clear natural boundaries: the Himalayan Mountains in the north, the Arabian Sea in the west, the Bay of Bengal in the east, and the Indian Ocean to the south. These geographical features remained relatively constant throughout history. The ancient Tamil poem mentioning 'the great mountain in the north' (Himalayas) and 'Cape Kumari' in the south shows that by 2,000 years ago, Indians understood their land's geographical extent. The Mahābhārata's list of regions (Kashmir, Haryana, Bengal, Assam, Kutch, Kerala) shows that despite political divisions, these were all understood as part of one cultural and geographical sphere.

content.title: Geography as Unifying Concept
content.text: The geographical concept of 'India' has been remarkably stable despite political fragmentation. Mountains, rivers, and ocean created natural barriers that made the Subcontinent a relatively distinct geographical region. This geographical unity allowed people in different regions to still see themselves as part of a larger Indian identity, even when politically separate.

content.title: Important Terms
items: Indian Subcontinent: The geographical region comprising modern India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, and parts of Afghanistan, bounded by the Himalayas, Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal, and Indian Ocean.
Sindhu/Indus: The major river of the northwest Subcontinent; the geographical feature through which foreigners first encountered and named India.
Sapta Sindhava: 'Land of seven rivers' — the name for the northwest region in the Rig Veda, one of the earliest documented names for part of India.
Bhāratavarṣha: 'Country of the Bharatas' — name for the entire Subcontinent in the Mahābhārata and later Sanskrit texts.
Jambudvīpa: 'Island of the jamun tree' — name for the Subcontinent referring to its geographical and botanical features.
Bhārata/Bharat: Evolved name for India, derived from Bhāratavarṣha, used in modern Sanskrit and Hindi respectively.
Hind/Hindu/Hindustān: Persian names derived from Sindhu; Hindustān combines Hindu + stān (Persian for 'land of').
India: English/Latin name derived through Greek (Indoi) from Persian Hindu; standard international name.
Aśhoka: Ancient Indian emperor (c. 250 BCE) who used Jambudvīpa to refer to his empire.
Constitution of India: Modern nation's founding document, adopted in 1950, which uses the phrase 'India, that is Bharat' to denote the nation.

glossary.definition: A large geographical region in South Asia bounded by the Himalayan Mountains in the north, the Arabian Sea in the west, the Bay of Bengal in the east, and the Indian Ocean to the south. It includes modern India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, and parts of Afghanistan.

The Sanskrit name for the Indus River, one of the major river systems of the Indian Subcontinent. This river was the primary geographical feature through which ancient foreigners encountered and named India.
A Sanskrit term meaning 'land of the seven rivers,' used in the Rig Veda (c. 1500 BCE) to refer to the northwest region of the Subcontinent with its river systems.

A Sanskrit term meaning 'country of the Bharatas,' used in the Mahābhārata to refer to the entire Indian Subcontinent. It represents one of the earliest unified concepts of 'India' by its own inhabitants.
A Sanskrit term meaning 'island of the jamun tree,' used in Indian texts including the Mahābhārata to refer to the Indian Subcontinent, emphasizing its geographical and botanical identity.
The modern form of Bhāratavarṣha, used as the official name for India in Sanskrit and Hindi respectively. It represents the indigenous, culturally-rooted identity of the nation.

Persian names for the region, derived from Sindhu (Indus River), used from the 6th century BCE onward when Persians controlled the Indus region. The word 'Hindu' originally denoted a geographical region, not a religion.
Greek adaptations of the Persian name 'Hindu,' used by ancient Greeks to refer to the region east of the Indus River.
The Latin/English name for the country, derived from Greek Indoi through historical linguistic evolution. It is the standard international name for the nation.

A Persian term combining 'Hindu' (the region) with '-stān' (meaning 'land of'), thus meaning 'land of the Hindus.' This term was used by Islamic rulers and became prominent during the medieval period.

An ancient Indian emperor (c. 250 BCE) who ruled over a vast empire that encompassed much of the Indian Subcontinent. His inscriptions provide historical evidence of the use of names like Jambudvīpa to refer to the entire region.
The founding legal document of the modern Indian nation-state, adopted in 1950, which establishes the structure of government and fundamental rights. It famously begins with 'India, that is Bharat.'
A Chinese Buddhist scholar and traveler (7th century CE) who traveled to India, studied with Indian scholars, collected Buddhist texts, and translated Sanskrit texts into Chinese upon his return.

summary_points: India has had many names throughout history, both by its own inhabitants and by foreign visitors. These names reflect the perspectives, experiences, and geographical understanding of those who used them. The multiplicity of names demonstrates India's long history, cultural diversity, and significance as a land that has been encountered and understood in different ways by different peoples.

Indians named their land based on cultural, religious, and geographical features. Early names include Sapta Sindhava (land of seven rivers) in the Rig Veda, Bhāratavarṣha (country of the Bharatas) and Jambudvīpa (island of the jamun tree) in the Mahābhārata. These names reflect the indigenous understanding of the Subcontinent as a unified cultural and geographical entity despite political fragmentation.

Foreigners typically named India based on the Indus River (Sindhu), which was their primary geographical contact point with the Subcontinent. The Persian word 'Hindu' (from Sindhu), adapted by Greeks as 'Indoi' and by Romans as 'India,' shows the etymological journey of how the region became known to the outside world. Names like 'Tianzhu' (heavenly master) by the Chinese reflect not just geographical encounter but also understanding of India's spiritual significance as the land of Buddhism.

The term 'Hindustān' emerged in medieval times, combining the Persian 'Hindu' with the suffix '-stān' (land of), reflecting Islamic rule's influence on the region's nomenclature. This demonstrates how the names for India evolved with different rulers and cultural encounters throughout different historical periods.

The modern Indian Constitution's opening phrase 'India, that is Bharat' perfectly encapsulates the nation's dual identity: India as the internationally recognized name (derived from foreign encounters) and Bharat as the indigenously rooted name (derived from ancient Sanskrit traditions). By using both names together, the Constitution acknowledges the continuity of India's ancient cultural identity while also its place in the modern international system.

The geographical concept of India has remained relatively stable throughout history, bounded by the Himalayas in the north, the Arabian Sea in the west, the Bay of Bengal in the east, and the Indian Ocean to the south. Despite political divisions and changing borders, these geographical boundaries created a sense of unity, allowing people across different regions to identify themselves as part of a larger Indian identity.

Ancient Indians demonstrated sophisticated geographical knowledge of their land. Tamil literary sources from 2,000 years ago mention the same geographical markers (the great mountain in the north, the ocean to south and west) as Sanskrit texts, showing that whether in north or south, Indians understood their land's extent through consistent geographical references.

The evolution of India's names (from Sapta Sindhava to Bhārata to Bharat to India) reflects the continuity of cultural memory combined with openness to external influences. This flexibility in naming while maintaining a core identity is characteristic of Indian civilization's ability to adapt and incorporate diverse influences while preserving continuity.
